question: Explain the fundamental difference between steganography and cryptography.
expectedPoints: Cryptography scrambles data to make it unreadable, protecting its content.
Steganography hides the very existence of the data within another innocent-looking carrier.
The goal of steganography is to avoid drawing attention, whereas encrypted data is obviously scrambled.

question: Describe how the Least Significant Bit (LSB) substitution method works for hiding data in a text file and discuss one of its major limitations.
expectedPoints: LSB modifies the least significant bit(s) of the binary values representing characters in a text file.
These subtle changes are often imperceptible to a human reader (e.g., changing a space character).
A major limitation is its vulnerability to detection; even minor formatting changes or re-saving the file can destroy the hidden message due to the low robustness of the method.

question: Compare and contrast syntactic and semantic methods for text steganography. Evaluate the key strengths and weaknesses of each approach in terms of capacity, robustness, and undetectability.
expectedPoints: Syntactic methods (e.g., modifying whitespace, punctuation) often offer high capacity and are simple to implement but are fragile and easily detected by analyzing file structure.
Semantic methods (e.g., using synonyms, linguistic rules, generating new text) manipulate the meaning of the text, resulting in higher undetectability and robustness against format changes.
A key weakness of semantic methods is lower capacity (bits hidden per word) and greater computational complexity for generation and analysis compared to syntactic methods.
